questions.question: Section 6 NFOATP 1997 creates an offence of injuring or threatening to injure with a syringe. What is the maximum penalty for a s.6(1) syringe attack on conviction on indictment?
questions.explanation: Section 6(1) NFOATP 1997 makes it an offence to injure or threaten to injure another using a syringe. On conviction on indictment, the maximum sentence is 10 years imprisonment. This reflects the severe and particular harm that can result from syringe attacks — not merely the physical wound inflicted by the syringe itself, but the psychological harm caused by the fear of blood-borne infection (HIV, hepatitis) from contact with the syringe, even if the syringe is subsequently found to be clean. Section 7(5) NFOATP 1997 provides that possession of a syringe in circumstances indicating intent to commit an offence is evidence of that intent. Options A, C, and D each state incorrect maximum sentences.

questions.question: Section 3A was inserted into the Non-Fatal Offences Against the Person Act 1997 by the Criminal Justice (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act 2023. When did this new offence come into force?
questions.explanation: Section 3A NFOATP 1997 (the standalone strangulation and suffocation offence) was inserted by s.22 of the Criminal Justice (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act 2023 (CJ(MP)A 2023). The CJ(MP)A 2023 came into force on 1 November 2023. This date is exam-critical: s.3A did not exist in Irish law before 1 November 2023. Prior to this date, strangulation had to be charged under s.3 assault causing harm or s.4 causing serious harm, both of which required proof of resulting harm. Section 3A makes the act of strangulation or suffocation itself the offence, regardless of whether harm results. Option A is incorrect. Option C (31 August 2024) is the commencement date for the Criminal Law (Sexual Offences and Human Trafficking) Act 2024 — a different statute. Option D is incorrect.

questions.question: Section 3A(1) NFOATP 1997 defines 'strangle' and 'suffocate.' Which of the following correctly states the statutory definition of 'strangle'?

options.A: 'Strangle' means wrapping hands around another's neck with intent to kill
options.B: 'Strangle' means applying force to the neck or throat of another in a way that impedes breathing or blood circulation
options.C: 'Strangle' means any act of physical force directed at another's airway
options.D: 'Strangle' means applying continuous pressure to the neck until loss of consciousness

questions.explanation: Section 3A (as inserted by CJ(MP)A 2023) defines 'strangle' as applying force to the neck or throat of another in a way that impedes breathing or blood circulation. Key points: (1) The force must be applied to the neck or throat; (2) The force must impede breathing or blood circulation — partial or complete impediment suffices; (3) There is no requirement of intent to kill or cause serious harm; (4) There is no requirement that the victim lose consciousness. This broad definition captures acts of non-fatal strangulation where the perpetrator applies pressure to the neck — a common form of domestic violence that is statistically linked to subsequent homicide. Option A adds an intent to kill requirement that is not in the section. Option C is overbroad (not all force to the airway constitutes strangling). Option D incorrectly requires loss of consciousness.

questions.question: Section 3A NFOATP 1997 also defines 'suffocate.' Which of the following correctly captures the statutory definition of suffocation?

options.A: Suffocate means blocking all breathing by any means, for any duration
options.B: Suffocate means asphyxiating another person by impeding breathing through covering the nose or mouth, constricting the chest or abdomen, or obstructing the airway with a foreign object
options.C: Suffocate means only the use of a pillow or similar soft object to restrict breathing
options.D: Suffocate means causing complete cessation of breathing leading to unconsciousness

questions.explanation: Section 3A NFOATP 1997 (as inserted by CJ(MP)A 2023) defines 'suffocate' as asphyxiating another person by impeding breathing through: (a) covering the nose or mouth; (b) constricting the chest or abdomen; or (c) obstructing the airway with a foreign object. The key feature is asphyxiation — the act must impede breathing, not merely restrict movement. This definition captures: smothering with a pillow; placing hands over nose and mouth; pressing on the chest to restrict breathing; and forcing objects into the airway. Critically, the definition does not require complete cessation of breathing or loss of consciousness (Option D is wrong) — partial impediment of breathing is sufficient. Option A is overbroad and not limited to the three specified mechanisms. Option C incorrectly limits the definition to soft objects.

questions.question: What is the maximum penalty for the s.3A strangulation and suffocation offence?

options.A: 10 years imprisonment — the same as s.3 assault causing harm post-2023

questions.explanation: Section 3A NFOATP 1997 carries a maximum penalty of life imprisonment on conviction on indictment. This exceptionally high maximum for a non-fatal offence reflects several policy considerations: (1) Strangulation is statistically one of the strongest predictors of subsequent homicide in domestic violence relationships; (2) Strangulation causes severe harm without necessarily leaving visible external marks, making prosecution under s.3/s.4 more difficult; (3) The Legislature intended to signal the utmost seriousness with which strangulation is treated. The life maximum makes s.3A equivalent to s.4 causing serious harm in terms of maximum sentence. Note that the life maximum does not mean life sentences will routinely be imposed — the maximum provides a ceiling for the most aggravated cases. Options A, B, and D state incorrect maximum sentences.

questions.question: Why did the legislature create s.3A as a standalone offence rather than relying on s.3 or s.4 NFOATP 1997 to prosecute strangulation?

options.A: S.3 and s.4 are exclusively for weapon-based attacks; s.3A was needed to cover non-weapon violence
options.B: The key gap filled by s.3A is that s.3 and s.4 both require proof of resulting harm; strangulation frequently causes no visible external marks or lasting injury (despite causing internal physiological harm and creating extreme danger); s.3A makes the act of strangulation or suffocation itself criminal, without requiring proof of resulting harm
options.C: S.3A was created because s.3 and s.4 could only be charged against adults; s.3A extends liability to juveniles
options.D: S.3A was created solely to address domestic violence — it cannot be charged in non-domestic contexts

questions.explanation: The legislative rationale for creating a standalone strangulation offence under s.3A is precisely that s.3 and s.4 both required proof of 'harm' or 'serious harm' respectively. Strangulation is notoriously difficult to prove under these provisions because: (1) External marks (bruising, petechiae) can be subtle, temporary, or absent; (2) Internal physiological effects (interrupted blood supply to the brain, respiratory damage) may not be immediately visible; (3) The victim's experience of terror and near-death may not satisfy the legal threshold of 'harm' to a body or mind in a provable sense. Section 3A resolves this by making the act of strangulation or suffocation itself the offence — proof of harm is not required. The prosecution need only prove the act of strangulation or suffocation. Option A is wrong about the scope of s.3/s.4. Option C invents a juvenile scope restriction. Option D incorrectly limits s.3A to domestic contexts — the section applies to any context.

questions.question: A defendant places their hands around a partner's neck during a domestic argument, squeezes for several seconds causing the partner to struggle to breathe, then releases. The partner has no visible injuries and does not require medical treatment. Can the defendant be charged under s.3A NFOATP 1997?

options.A: No — without visible injury or medical treatment, no offence under the NFOATP can be proved
options.B: Yes — the defendant applied force to the partner's neck in a way that impeded breathing (the partner struggled to breathe), satisfying the definition of 'strangle' under s.3A; no harm or injury is required to be proved; the act itself constitutes the offence
options.C: No — s.3A requires the strangulation to be sustained for at least 30 seconds before it constitutes a criminal act
options.D: Yes — but only because the context is domestic; the same act in a non-domestic setting would be charged under s.3

questions.explanation: On these facts, s.3A is clearly applicable: (1) The defendant applied force to the partner's neck (using their hands); (2) That force impeded the partner's breathing (the partner struggled to breathe) — satisfying the statutory definition of 'strangle'; (3) No harm or injury is required — s.3A does not incorporate a harm requirement; the act of strangulation is the offence. The absence of visible injury or medical treatment is irrelevant to the s.3A charge. This is precisely the gap the legislature sought to fill: pre-s.3A, this scenario would have been difficult to charge under s.3 (no visible harm) or s.4 (no serious harm established). Option A incorrectly requires visible injury. Option C invents a 30-second duration requirement that has no statutory basis. Option D incorrectly limits s.3A to domestic contexts.

questions.question: Section 7(5) NFOATP 1997 provides that possession of a syringe in particular circumstances is evidence of a particular intent. What does this provision establish?

options.A: Possession of a syringe is a strict liability offence under s.7(5) — no mens rea is required
options.B: Section 7(5) provides that possession of a syringe in circumstances suggesting intent to commit an offence is evidence from which a court may infer that intent — it creates an evidential inference, not an irrebuttable presumption
options.C: Section 7(5) makes it an offence to possess a syringe in a public place in any circumstances
options.D: Section 7(5) reverses the burden of proof entirely — once possession of a syringe is proved, the accused must prove innocence beyond reasonable doubt

questions.explanation: Section 7(5) NFOATP 1997 creates an evidential provision: where a person is found in possession of a syringe in circumstances that suggest an intention to use it to commit an offence, that possession is evidence from which the court may infer the requisite intent. This is an evidential inference provision, not a strict liability offence. It does not reverse the legal burden of proof or create an irrebuttable presumption — it is simply an aid to proof, allowing the court to draw inferences from the circumstances of possession. The accused remains able to provide an innocent explanation for possession (e.g., diabetic requiring insulin injection). The constitutional principle that the prosecution must prove guilt beyond reasonable doubt is preserved. Option A incorrectly characterises the provision as strict liability. Option C sweeps too broadly — legitimate medical possession is obviously not prohibited. Option D mischaracterises the standard of proof.
